Revised paper sections submitted by the author:
[Revised EXPERIMENTAL SETUP]
In this section, we provide a detailed description of our experimental setup to enhance reproducibility. We utilized a synthetic dataset generated from a causal Bayesian network (CBN) with known causal relationships. The dataset consisted of 10,000 samples, with 5 features representing different environmental variables. We employed a range of parameter settings for our algorithms, including learning rates of 0.01, 0.001, and 0.0001, and evaluated performance using metrics such as average regret and causal discovery accuracy. Our baselines included state-of-the-art methods in causal inference and domain generalization, specifically [list specific methods], which were selected based on their relevance to our research question. We ensured that all experiments were conducted under the same conditions to facilitate fair comparisons.

[Revised EMPIRICAL RESULTS ANALYSIS]

revised_sections.content: In this section, we provide a detailed description of our experimental setup to enhance reproducibility. We utilized a synthetic dataset generated from a causal Bayesian network (CBN) with known causal relationships. The dataset consisted of 10,000 samples, with 5 features representing different environmental variables. We employed a range of parameter settings for our algorithms, including learning rates of 0.01, 0.001, and 0.0001, and evaluated performance using metrics such as average regret and causal discovery accuracy. Our baselines included state-of-the-art methods in causal inference and domain generalization, specifically [list specific methods], which were selected based on their relevance to our research question. We ensured that all experiments were conducted under the same conditions to facilitate fair comparisons.

In this revised section, we present a comprehensive analysis of our empirical results. We conducted additional ablation studies to assess the impact of various components of our approach on performance. For instance, we varied the degree of causal knowledge available to the agents and observed how this affected their ability to generalize across domains. Our findings indicate that while agents with complete causal models performed best, those with approximate causal knowledge still achieved competitive results, particularly in low-regret scenarios. We also included sensitivity analyses to explore how changes in parameter settings influenced the outcomes, providing a more robust validation of our theoretical claims.

We have revised our discussion of the assumptions underlying Theorem 1 to clarify their implications in practical scenarios. We acknowledge that the assumption of optimality may not hold in all real-world applications. Therefore, we explored alternative settings where agents operate under suboptimal conditions. Our analysis suggests that while the theoretical guarantees may weaken, agents can still learn useful causal relationships even when not operating at optimality. This finding highlights the potential for our approach to be applicable in a broader range of scenarios, thereby enhancing its relevance to real-world applications.

Our findings indicate that while agents with complete causal models performed best, those with approximate causal knowledge still achieved competitive results, particularly in low-regret scenarios. We also included sensitivity analyses to explore how changes in parameter settings influenced the outcomes, providing a more robust validation of our theoretical claims.

We have revised our discussion of the assumptions underlying Theorem 1 to clarify their implications in practical scenarios.
